dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly unifies several memory modalities (explicit knowledge graphs, retrieval-augmented episodic memory, compressed/summarized representations) into a single design principle for LLM agents in text games, arguing that structured, task-specific, polymodal memory yields resource-efficient action selection, robust recovery, cross-instance generalization, and multi-agent coherence.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not advance or evaluate this unifying memory-centric framework; it focuses on task decomposition and ToT rather than integrating or comparing knowledge-graph, retrieval, and compression approaches under one principle. The proposed unification is therefore not present in the paper.

unification.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper unifies and compares two concrete knowledge modalities (affordances and short-term correct-action memory) and multiple injection strategies across RL and LM paradigms within a single empirical framework, providing direct comparisons of representation choices (concatenation, separate encoder, KG insertion).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory asserts a higher-level unification of three canonical memory modalities—explicit semantic/episodic KGs, retrieval-augmented episodic memory (experience/reflection banks), and compressed/summarized representations—into a single structured, task-specific, polymodal memory architecture for LLM agents, and claims joint benefits for planning, recovery, generalization, and multi-agent coherence.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper covers only subsets of those modalities (KG-like affordances and short-term action memory; it does not implement retrieval-augmented memory or systematic compression strategies), so the three-way conceptual unification and the claim that their joint use yields the broad cognitive benefits are not presented or validated here.

unification.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates MC-DML relative to prior lines (knowledge-graph state representations, case-based reasoning, value-sharing semantics, LLM-as-prior MCTS, reflection agents) and implements LLM+MCTS with short-term and episodic reflection memory, but does not integrate other memory modalities such as semantic/episodic knowledge graphs or compressed summaries in a single system.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly proposes unifying multiple memory modalities (explicit semantic/episodic knowledge graphs, retrieval-augmented episodic banks, compressed/summarized histories) into a structured, task-specific, polymodal memory architecture to broadly supercharge LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The multi-component unification (KGs + retrieval banks + compressed summaries as a single, task-tailored memory architecture) is not implemented or evaluated in the paper; that conceptual unification goes beyond the paper's contributions.

unification.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates CALM relative to prior approaches (heuristic agents, admissible-action handicaps, n-gram/seq2seq affordance models, KG-augmented RL) and empirically demonstrates that an LM proposer plus RL re-ranker can subsume some benefits of admissible-action access in practice.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly unifies multiple memory modalities—semantic/episodic knowledge graphs, retrieval-augmented episodic stores, and compressed/summarized representations—into a single organizing principle for improving LLM agents' planning and long-horizon performance.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not propose or empirically evaluate a combined architecture that integrates these distinct memory modalities or demonstrate that they are instances of a single underlying principle; that explicit unification is absent from the paper.

unification.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates its approach relative to prior methods (knowledge-graph agents, attention/transformer architectures, action-generation LMs) and empirically contrasts a pre-trained-LM approach with KG/attention architectures, showing that LM priors can match or exceed some structured-KG methods on several games.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly unifies three distinct memory families (semantic/episodic knowledge graphs, retrieval-augmented episodic memory, compressed/summarized representations) into a single design principle: structured, task-specific, polymodal memory architectures composed appropriately will substantially improve planning, generalization, and multi-agent coherence.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not propose or defend this three-part taxonomy or claim that those memory families are instances of a single organizing principle; it only contrasts LM priors with KGs and cites prior work, so the explicit unification/taxonomy is absent from the paper.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Most prior augmentation/retrieval papers evaluate on one or a narrow set of tasks; the community recognizes transfer of memory methods across tasks is desirable but not guaranteed.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: MemInsight demonstrates the same autonomous attribute-augmentation and retrieval abstraction works across multiple dialogue-oriented tasks and datasets (LLM-REDIAL for recommendation, LoCoMo for QA and summarization), and across both attribute- and embedding-based retrieval modes.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This is an explicit empirical scope expansion showing applicability of attribute-augmented memory beyond a single benchmark, but the generalization is limited to text-dialogue domains and the specific datasets/models evaluated.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper evaluates Sweet&Sour only on the ScienceWorld benchmark and reports that the method is broadly applicable in principle to interactive text environments, but empirical validation is limited to that single environment and to single-agent tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: Authors claim applicability to other interactive text environments and to augmentations like grounding or gradient-based learning, and they show gains across model sizes, especially for smaller models, suggesting some robustness across computational scales.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The theory expands scope to many game domains (ALFWorld, WebShop, Jubensha, multi-agent social simulation, e-commerce, open-ended role-play) and specific modalities of structured memory (KGs, compressed summaries) that the paper does not test; that expansion is proposed but not empirically demonstrated in the paper.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The survey covers a broad set of environments and game genres (adventure, sandbox, simulation, role-playing, strategy, action) and argues that memory systems (especially long-term retrieval) are central to cross-episode and cross-instance performance; it documents methods aimed at making memory operate across episodes and domains (e.g., streaming memory trees, parametric fine-tuning, skill libraries).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory scopes the claim specifically to LLM-based planning agents in multi-task/multi-domain text games and emphasizes retrieval-augmented episodic memory as a key enabler of analogical generalization across instance/task boundaries (one-shot or variable tasks).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already advertises the importance of memory for generalization and catalogs multiple works that attempt cross-episode reuse, but it does not assert or validate a broad, consistently replicable generalization guarantee across the full set of listed domains; the theory's focused claim about consistent analogical transfer in one-shot/variable tasks is a strengthening of the survey's more cautious coverage.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates that prompting + symbolic modules generalizes across four symbolic tasks and across two LLM families (GPT-3.5 and GPT-4) within the TextWorldExpress benchmarks, indicating cross-task applicability of the neurosymbolic prompting approach.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory expands the scope to claim that structured, task-specific and polymodal memory systems—especially retrieval-augmented episodic memory—will improve LLM agents broadly across multi-task/multi-domain text games, one-shot/variable tasks, creative exploration, and multi-agent social reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This broad domain- and task-level generalization to many game families and multi-agent settings via specific memory architectures is not evaluated in the paper; it is a plausible but nontrivial extrapolation beyond the paper's benchmarks.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: RAP generalizes retrieval-augmented episodic memory from text-only tasks to multimodal embodied tasks and demonstrates transfer of constructed memory across evaluation models; the paper also explores environment-adaptive retrieval (action vs observation, intra-task signals) and varying retriever components.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The broader theory claims additional scope expansions—structured task-specific memory types (knowledge graphs, compressed summaries), benefits for long-horizon planning, exploration, multi-agent social reasoning, and broad cross-instance analogical generalization beyond the evaluated benchmarks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper empirically demonstrates text→multimodal and cross-model transfer for episodic retrieval, but it does not implement or evaluate other structured memory types (semantic graphs, compressed summaries) or extensive multi-agent/social-role scenarios claimed in the theory; those broader scope claims are therefore extensions not validated here.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper's experiments and claims are scoped to a KG-A2C-based thespian agent evaluated in the LIGHT text-roleplaying environment, demonstrating multi-character policy decomposition and few-shot blending within that environment.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends the claim to LLM-based and planning agents across many multi-task/multi-domain text-game settings (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld, Jubensha, social simulation, e-commerce, open-ended role-play), arguing that structured, task-specific memory (including retrieval) enables cross-instance analogical generalization and multi-agent coherence.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not test other environments, larger LLM planning agents, or retrieval-augmented episodic memories across domains; therefore the broader scope expansion claimed by the theory is not demonstrated and remains an unsupported extrapolation here.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates the action-injection technique generalizes across four different text-game tasks and many parametric variations within the text-game domain, under the constraints of gold trajectories and a valid-action aid.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory expands the claim to a broader scope: structured polymodal memory systems (KGs + retrieval episodic memory + compressed summaries) will supercharge LLM agents across multi-task/multi-domain text games, including one-shot/analogical transfer and multi-agent social reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides evidence that external symbolic tools can improve performance across multiple tasks, but it does not demonstrate or analyze retrieval-augmented episodic memory, compressed-summary scaling, or multi-agent/one-shot analogical transfer; extending to those broader domains is not established here and requires further work.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates zero-shot generalization of the Worldformer across nine unseen Jericho text games and discusses domain-specific failure modes (e.g., label imbalance for valid actions) and tractability gains from predicting smaller graph-diffs.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory expands scope beyond these experiments by asserting that structured, task-specific polymodal memory (including retrieval-augmented episodic memory) will enable superior generalization, planning, and multi-agent coherence across many text-game-like domains and one-shot/creative tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the Worldformer results to retrieval-enabled LLM agents and claiming broad cross-domain and multi-agent generalization is not tested here; while the paper's results hint that structured representations help, the retrieval-augmented, polymodal extension is not demonstrated and must be treated as a new scope expansion.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper's empirical claims and evaluation are scoped to the ALFWorld benchmark (including human-annotated goals within that environment) and center on language-model fine-tuning plus IAM; it does not empirically evaluate across the broad set of other text-game domains listed in the theory.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly expands scope: it claims the retrieval-augmented episodic-memory effect holds across multi-task/multi-domain text games (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld, Jubensha, social simulation, e-commerce, role-play) and supports cross-instance analogical generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the IAM-like benefit observed in ALFWorld to a claim that retrieval-augmented episodic memory will produce large, often superlinear gains across many diverse text-game domains is not demonstrated here; the broader cross-domain applicability is an extrapolation beyond the paper's single-benchmark evidence.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Experiments are confined to a Chinese Jubensha dataset and the ThinkThrice architecture; authors state the framework is flexible and could be adapted to other interactive contexts but provide no cross-domain empirical tests.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper suggests potential transferability of the pipeline to other multi-agent or text-game environments but does not empirically extend or validate the results beyond Jubensha.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The broader theory's claim that retrieval-augmented episodic memory produces large (often superlinear) gains across diverse text-game domains and supports analogical in-context learning across instance/task boundaries is not demonstrated here; cross-domain generalization remains untested.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates generalization of a learned belief-graph agent across hundreds of TextWorld instances and difficulty levels, showing robustness of a single policy across a distribution of games and reporting transfer/mixture experiments (level 5).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends the claim to a broader scope: LLM-based and planning agents across many multimodal/multi-domain text-game-like environments (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld, social simulation, e‑commerce, open-ended role-play) and to memory modalities (retrieval, summaries) that support in-context analogical reasoning and one-shot transfer.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extension of the GATA-style graph-memory result to LLM-based planners, to retrieval-augmented episodic stores, to other benchmarks and multi-agent social reasoning is not demonstrated in the paper; the cross-domain, LLM-centric and polymodal-memory generalization is an extrapolation beyond the paper's empirical scope.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates applicability across three distinct domains (sequential decision-making in AlfWorld, multi-hop reasoning in HotPotQA, and code generation in HumanEval/MBPP/LeetcodeHard) and documents domain-specific failure modes (e.g., WebShop), showing the approach generalizes beyond a single task type.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory claims a broader scope: that structured, task-specific and polymodal memory architectures (knowledge graphs, retrieval-augmented episodic memory, compressed summaries) will supercharge LLM agents across many text-game settings including social simulation, e-commerce, open-ended role-play and multi-agent reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper establishes multi-domain generalization for the Reflexion paradigm but does not empirically evaluate the fuller suite of structured/polymodal memory architectures or extensive multi-agent social/e-commerce settings invoked by the theory; the broader scope is a plausible extrapolation rather than an explicit result of this paper.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper shows diff history benefits across two procedurally generated environments (BabyAI-Text and NetHack), analyzes scaling with more demonstrations and dependence on pretraining, and discusses environment-structure dependence (helps when observations have line-structured changes).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory expands the claim to a broader class of structured-memory interventions (KGs, retrieval stores, compressed summaries) and to many multi-task, multimodal, and multi-agent text-game domains, asserting that structured, task-specific, polymodal memory architectures systematically improve LLM agents across instance/task boundaries.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper supports the narrower principle that structuring prompt/memory inputs can help, but it does not empirically validate the full breadth of memory types, modalities, or multi-agent scenarios claimed by the theory; the broader scope extension remains to be tested and is a plausible synthesis rather than an in-paper result.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper evaluates AriGraph across multiple interactive text-game tasks, NetHack with restricted observations, and multi-hop QA, demonstrating cross-task applicability within text-based and textual QA domains and robustness to increased environment complexity.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a further scope expansion: that retrieval-augmented episodic memories will generalize across many multi-task/multi-domain text games (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld, social simulation, e-commerce, role-play) and support in-context analogical reasoning across instance/task boundaries.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper supports generalization within the tested family of text-game and QA tasks, but does not empirically cover several of the additional domains named by the theory; the broader domain claims are therefore plausible extrapolations but are not directly established in the paper.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The authors argue (discussion/future work) that the architecture should generalize to other interactive settings (role-play, VR, social prototyping) and to multimodal or larger LMs, but the empirical evaluation is limited to the Smallville sandbox and 25-agent simulations.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper introduces design choices (natural-language memory stream, reflection trees, retrieval scoring) that are domain-agnostic in principle and thus claimed to be applicable to broader LLM-agent text-game domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Cross-domain generalization to many named text-game environments and the claim of large or superlinear gains across those settings is not demonstrated; it is a plausible extrapolation but remains unstated/untested in the paper and must be inferred from the single-domain results.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: KG-A2C is evaluated across a diverse set of human-authored Jericho IF games, showing that the KG+template approach generalizes across genres, game structures, and reward functions within parser-based interactive-fiction environments; hyperparameters were held fixed across games to demonstrate broad applicability inside this domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory expands scope beyond the paper to claim applicability to LLM-based and planning agents across multi-task/multi-domain text games (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld, social simulation, role-play), emphasizing cross-instance/task analogical transfer via retrieval-augmented episodic memory and compressed representations for scale.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the KG+template findings from single-game-per-training IF experiments to LLM-based, multi-task, one-shot, and multimodal text-game settings (and asserting retrieval-augmented memory will deliver systemic gains there) is not supported or derived by the paper; this scope expansion is novel relative to the paper.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper extends KG-A2C and Story Shaping to a MoE framework for role-transfer within the LIGHT open-world environment and reports results on blended and partial-blend target roles, demonstrating few-shot transfer within that setting.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes from these kinds of techniques to claim applicability to LLM-based and planning agents across many text-game domains (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld, Jubensha, social simulation, e-commerce, role-play) and emphasizes memory types (retrieval-augmented episodic, compressed summaries) as the key enablers of cross-instance/task generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper experiments are confined to KG-A2C agents in LIGHT and do not test LLM-based agents, retrieval-augmented episodic memory, compressed-memory primitives, or the wide set of domains named by the theory; the cross-architecture and multi-domain scope expansion asserted by the theory is not supported or derived from this paper's results.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: This paper's evaluations and claims are scoped to a single, complex non-English, imperfect-information cooperative environment (Guandan) and include ablations over TopK, language, and ToM order; it does not empirically validate approaches across multiple distinct text-game domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts that structured, task-specific and polymodal memory architectures (including retrieval-augmented episodic memory and compressed histories) provide resource-efficient planning, robust recovery, and generalization across many text-game domains (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld, social simulation, role-play) and across task/instance boundaries.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Cross-domain and multi-task generalization claims via retrieval-augmented polymodal memory are not supported or explored by the paper's Guandan-only experiments; thus the theory's scope expansion goes beyond the paper's demonstrated evidence.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates limited generalization: fine-tuning on data from 4x4 boards and evaluating on 10x10 boards yields improved performance, and the authors suggest extension to other game types as future work. Experimental validation is confined to a single game (Goedendag) and board-size transfer.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory claims broad applicability across many multi-task/multi-domain text-game settings (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld, Jubensha, social simulation, e-commerce, open-ended role-play) and emphasizes retrieval-augmented episodic memory as the main enabler of cross-instance in-context analogical reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the empirical claim to that broad set of domains and attributing cross-domain generalization primarily to retrieval-augmented episodic memory is not supported by the paper's experiments; cross-domain scope and retrieval-driven transfer are not implemented or evaluated here.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Experiments are limited to ScienceWorld (ten tasks / chosen subtasks) and the paper explicitly highlights that gains are task-dependent and sometimes negative when injected knowledge is irrelevant; authors discuss that benefits depend on task relevance and representation.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory extends claims to broader domains: multi-task/multi-domain text games, variable/one-shot tasks, creative/explorative requirements, and multi-agent hypothesis formation where memory must generalize across instance/task boundaries.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not evaluate multi-domain generalization, true one-shot transfer, creative open-ended tasks, or multi-agent settings, nor does it implement cross-instance analogical retrieval; therefore the theory's expanded scope is not supported and represents an extension beyond the reported experiments.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper's experiments and claims are scoped to single-agent text-based games from the Jericho benchmark using gpt-3.5-turbo with specific memory/window sizes and PUCT settings; results are empirical within that domain and configuration.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends the claim to a much broader scope (multiple domains like ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld, e-commerce, open-ended role-play, multimodal/polymodal memory, and multi-agent social reasoning) where memory must generalize across tasks and support analogical in-context reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Those broader domain and modality extensions are not evidenced by the paper; the cross-domain, multimodal, and multi-agent generalization is therefore a scope expansion that is not supported by the reported experiments.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates cross-game generalization: a single CALM model trained on ClubFloyd transcripts generalizes to 28 unseen Jericho games and shows sensitivity analyses (pretraining, dataset size, top-k) that characterize transfer behavior across those single-player text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory claims a broader scope: that structured, task-specific and polymodal memory architectures (including retrieval-augmented episodic memory and compressed summaries) will enable resource-efficient action selection, robust recovery, generalization across instance/task boundaries and multi-agent coherence across many domains (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld, social simulation, etc.).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper establishes generalization of an LM proposer across many single-player text games, it does not evaluate the broader classes of structured memories, multimodal inputs, or multi-agent/other-domain settings named in the theory; the theory's scope expansion therefore goes beyond the paper's demonstrated domain.

generalization_scope_expansion.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates some scope expansion for LM priors: DistilBERT fine-tuned on gameplay transcripts from other games transfers to held-out Jericho games, improves sample efficiency, and yields measurable transfer (small experiment Zork1→Zork3) compared to baselines, indicating better cross-instance generalization than training from scratch.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory extends scope further, claiming retrieval-augmented episodic memory will enable robust in-context analogical reasoning and large (often superlinear) gains in multi-task and multi-domain text-game families (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld, social simulations, open-ended role-play) and in one-shot or highly variable tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the paper's LM-prior empirical results to the broad, cross-domain claim about retrieval-augmented episodic memory is not supported by experiments in this paper; the multi-domain, one-shot, and retrieval-dependent generalization claims go beyond the evaluated Jericho games and are not demonstrated here.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents limitations: Chinese-language dataset specificity, stochastic variability of LLM outputs, sensitivity to model versions and cost, and dependence of agent success on the quantity/quality of retrieved information and SR/SV thresholds.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It empirically shows that absent MR agents perform poorly at acquiring others' facts and that SR/SV parameters and retrieval effectiveness materially affect answer authenticity and downstream performance.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: These constraints align with the theory's caveats (reduced gains for tasks with no analogy/transfer potential and dependence on retrieval quality) and are explicitly established in the paper rather than being newly discovered limitations.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents constraints and failure modes: injected knowledge can harm performance when irrelevant, benefits vary strongly by task and model, and the injection modality matters (KG insertion typically outperforms naive concatenation or a separate encoder in their ablations).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory also notes caveats—tasks with no analogy/transfer potential may see little benefit and effectiveness depends on retrieval quality and selection—which align with the paper's observed sensitivity to relevance and representation.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: These limitations are empirically observed and discussed in the paper, so the cautionary constraints matching the theory's special cases are already explicitly established by the paper's results.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper documents limitations: Reflexion can stick in local minima, is sensitive to memory window size and prompt/context limits, depends on the quality of self-evaluation and generated unit tests, and fails to improve on tasks requiring very diverse/creative exploration (WebShop experiments).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory lists analogous caveats (limited gains when no transfer/analogy exists; dependence on retrieval quality, embedding/weighting, plan/window selection).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: These constraints are explicitly discussed and partially empirically evidenced in the paper; the theory's special-case qualifications largely restate the paper's documented limitations and are not new relative to it.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper documents limitations and conditions: performance depends on LLM/backbone quality and triplet extraction accuracy, episodic retrieval weighting matters (they specify a relevance scoring), and episodic memory can be less useful or require different handling in tasks emphasizing frequent updates or low-information observations (e.g., Cleaning).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly lists related special cases (limited gain in fully deterministic/fixed tasks) and notes dependence of effectiveness on retrieval quality (embeddings, weighting, plan/window selection).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: These constraints are already articulated and empirically supported in the paper (ablation and analysis showing LLM quality and episodic vs semantic tradeoffs), so the paper contains these limitations explicitly rather than introducing new, independent constraints beyond its experiments.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The survey explicitly discusses limitations and failure modes: memory effectiveness depends on retrieval quality (embeddings, weighting, selection), there are trade-offs (latency vs reasoning depth for action games), consolidation and write-back decisions are nontrivial, noisy perception and partial observability constrain retrieval utility, and purely parametric memories vs external memories have different trade-offs.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory includes special-case caveats that tasks with no transfer potential gain less and that effectiveness depends on retrieval quality (embedding, weighting, plan/window selection).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: These constraints are explicitly present and discussed in the paper; the theory's caveats mirror the paper's documented limitations and are not novel relative to the survey.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents limitations and failure modes: retrieval failures and incomplete recall, occasional embellishments/hallucinations, mis-selection of locations or social norms, sensitivity to retrieval quality and hyperparameters, high token/computational cost, and vulnerability to prompt/memory hacking and underlying LLM biases.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It provides diagnostic examples and a qualitative analysis of when and why the architecture breaks (e.g., wrong retrievals, poor grounding of location norms, instruction-tuning artifacts leading to over-cooperativeness), and suggests mitigations and future directions.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Identification and demonstration of these specific constraints for the proposed memory+reflection+planning pipeline in a multi-agent sandbox setting are explicit contributions of the paper.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly reports practical limitations: LLM agents exhibit memory-related failures (e.g., forgetting retrieved routes in MapReader, limited capacity hurting Sorting), remain error-prone, and benefit from constrained prompts; it discusses some task-dependent inefficiencies.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory highlights constraints such as reduced benefit on tasks with no analogy/transfer potential and a dependence of effectiveness on retrieval quality (embeddings, weighting, selection), prescribing retrieval-quality as a necessary factor.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Memory capacity and forgetting are explicit failure modes in the paper, but the paper does not analyze or quantify retrieval-quality dependencies or the no-transfer special case; those explicit retrieval-quality caveats and the formulated special-case limitation are not analyzed and therefore are peripheral refinements.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper documents practical limitations and sensitivities: retrieval-key and retriever design, embedding choice (sentence-transformers vs CLIP), windowed trajectory selection, environment-dependent weighting, and recursive trial depth (d_max) affect performance; WebShop requires special intra-task considerations and memory construction quality matters for transfer.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory's caveats (diminished gains when there is no transferable analogy, sensitivity to retrieval quality/embedding/selection/windowing) mirror limitations ablated and discussed in the paper.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: These failure modes and engineering constraints are empirically recognized and partially analyzed in the paper (ablation tables, retriever variants, transfer-memory experiments), so the limitation claims are present but treated as implementation-dependent considerations rather than a new theoretical failure regime.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper and its experiments show that augmentation quality depends on the backbone LLM used for attribute extraction, that augmentations can be overly generic in ambiguous contexts, and that some question categories (temporal/adversarial) may see smaller or no gains versus DPR.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: MemInsight explicitly identifies practical failure modes and dependencies: sensitivity to augmentation/retrieval quality, occasional generic (low-specificity) attributes, reliance on capable augmentation models (e.g., Claude-3-Sonnet performed better), and current restriction to text-only inputs.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The detailed, experiment-backed listing of these operational constraints and the empirical demonstration of cases where DPR matches or outperforms MemInsight are explicitly reported but align with expected limitations of retrieval-augmentation systems.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents limitations and failure regimes: when no expert contains relevant knowledge the MoE reduces to learning-from-scratch and performance degrades, distractor (irrelevant) experts can slow early learning, and value-seeding can produce premature convergence/expert starvation if not mitigated.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory lists related special cases (little benefit for fully deterministic/fixed tasks) and adds retrieval-specific constraints (effectiveness depends on retrieval quality: embeddings, weighting, plan/window selection).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already establishes and empirically supports the general constraint that gains diminish when there is no transfer and that irrelevant experts can harm learning; however, retrieval-quality dependencies (embeddings/weighting/selection) specific to retrieval-augmented episodic memory are not discussed or tested in the paper, so those retrieval-specific constraints are novel relative to this work.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly acknowledges limitations: results are from a single environment (ScienceWorld), LLMs do not guarantee reasoning correctness, performance varies with model size, and sampling only failures degrades performance. It also notes hard tasks where all methods struggle.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: Sweet&Sour explicitly notes practical constraints (dependence on memory management, retrieval relevance, and LLM reasoning reliability) and reports empirical failure cases and decreased gains on particularly hard tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The theory's articulated constraints (e.g., little gain on tasks with no transfer/analogy potential; dependence on retrieval quality and selection algorithms) largely echo the paper's peripheral caveats and are not novel relative to the paper's stated limitations.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly discusses limitations: LLM input-window constraints, the chosen in-trial short-window may miss long-ago clues, dependence on quality of LLM outputs/log-prob estimates, and practical limits on cross-trial memory size and retrieval selection.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory enumerates related constraints and special cases (tasks with no transfer/analogy potential may benefit less; effectiveness depends on retrieval quality, embedding/weighting, and plan/window selection).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: These practical limitations and dependencies are already acknowledged and partially studied (ablation of memory components) in the paper; the theory's caveats largely restate and slightly generalize those practical concerns rather than introduce new formal constraints.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents limitations: dependence on pretrained LM and ClubFloyd data (w/o pretraining performance collapses), sensitivity to top-k (precision/recall tradeoff), many games remain unsolved, and DRRN sometimes fails to fully exploit high-scoring exploration trajectories produced under CALM.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory lists related constraints and special cases (e.g., smaller gains on deterministic/no-analogy tasks and sensitivity to retrieval quality such as embeddings and window selection) as part of its scoping statements.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already reports several task-dependent limitations overlapping the theory's cautions, but it does not evaluate retrieval-quality dependencies because it does not implement retrieval-augmented episodic memory; retrieval-specific failure modes remain untested in the paper.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly identifies operational constraints: access to valid-action supervision is critical (KG-A2C-unsupervised fails), template-based action spaces are necessary for tractable exploration (token-level decoding performs poorly), and the knowledge-graph components primarily accelerate learning though per-game gains vary. It also notes practical implementation limits (e.g., reliance on Jericho's valid-action detection and handcrafted template availability).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory lists constraints such as diminished benefit for tasks with no transfer/analogy potential and sensitivity of effectiveness to retrieval quality (embeddings, weighting, window/plan selection).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not analyze retrieval-quality dependence or the specific special-case that retrieval yields little benefit when tasks lack analogical structure; those constraints are not reported here and are reasonable consequences of retrieval-based systems but are not explicitly established in the paper.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper documents limitations and constraints relevant to its approach: partial observability and combinatorial action spaces make learning hard; extractive QA methods can over-extract and fail when descriptions are hidden; performance correlates with average valid-action counts and label imbalance; predicting graph-diffs is empirically easier than full-graph prediction.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory specifies retrieval-specific constraints: reduced benefit when tasks lack analogy/transfer potential (fully deterministic/fixed tasks) and sensitivity of effectiveness to retrieval quality (embeddings, weighting, plan/window selection).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Those retrieval-specific failure modes are not analyzed or reported in the paper; they are plausible and predictable from general ML considerations but are not present in this work and thus represent novel, unstated constraints relative to the paper.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly reports important limitations: the proposed multi-agent ToT pipeline has much higher LM token cost than single-model baselines, fine-tuning was limited by compute/resources, and some components (area selection, summarization) are critical to reduce errors but add complexity; the authors also mention constructing a memory bank as future work to improve long-term memorization.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory enumerates constraints for retrieval-based approaches: reduced benefit when tasks have no analogy/transfer potential and sensitivity of effectiveness to retrieval quality (embeddings, weighting, plan/window selection).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The specific constraints about retrieval-sensitivity and no-analogy regimes are not empirically investigated in the paper (retrieval is not implemented), though the paper's cost/complexity caveats align with the general idea that architectural choices trade performance for cost. The retrieval-specific limitations are inferable but not tested.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper notes practical limitations and failure modes: performance depends on the amount of demonstrations (sample-efficiency limits), context-window/token length truncation (sliding window), and that IAM only augments training with successful trajectories (hindsight relabeling is non-trivial in ALFWorld). It also reports variability across seeds and a remaining gap between partial and fully supervised models.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory highlights different constraints: diminished gains on tasks with no analogy/transfer potential (fully deterministic/fixed tasks) and sensitivity to retrieval quality (embeddings, weighting, window/plan selection) as necessary conditions for the retrieval-memory law to hold.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Constraints tied specifically to retrieval quality and analogy potential are not analyzed in the paper; those are additional, substantive limitations absent from the paper's experimental or theoretical treatment.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper documents concrete limitations: inferred belief graphs are noisy and denser than ground-truth (interpretable but not sparse), discrete updater variants accumulate more errors and are vulnerable to round-off/operation errors, and there is a substantial gap between learned belief graphs and the ground-truth-graph upper bound; performance depends on pretraining and graph quality.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory lists constraints specific to retrieval-augmented episodic memory: diminished gains on tasks with no analogy/transfer potential (fully deterministic, fixed tasks) and sensitivity of effectiveness to retrieval quality (embeddings, weighting, plan/window selection).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Task-class and retrieval-quality constraints are not discussed experimentally in the paper (the paper focuses on graph-noise and updater error accumulation); while the general theme that memory quality matters aligns with the paper, the retrieval-specific limitations are not analyzed here.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents limitations and failure modes: diff history requires structured textual observations to be effective, can increase token count in low-dimensional settings (e.g., BabyAI-Text), and its benefits interact with pretraining and auxiliary objectives; these are empirically demonstrated and discussed in the limitations.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory lists additional caveats specific to retrieval-augmented systems (e.g., diminished gains in fully deterministic/no-transfer tasks and sensitivity to retrieval quality, embedding/weighting, and selection of plan/window), and notes dependence of effectiveness on retrieval performance.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Constraints tied specifically to retrieval-system engineering (embedding quality, retrieval selection/weighting) are not analyzed in the paper and therefore are novel relative to this work; general domain-dependence (need for structured observations) is already present in the paper.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly notes limitations and constraints: gains are heterogeneous across games, the approach does not solve exploration (curiosity-driven methods remain necessary), agents can still overfit/memorize under some handicaps, and IF games remain far from solved despite LM priors.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory lists task- and retrieval-specific constraints: limited benefit for tasks with no transfer/analogy potential (fully deterministic/fixed tasks) and sensitivity of effectiveness to retrieval quality (embeddings, weighting, plan/window selection).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper documents heterogeneous gains and exploration limits but does not analyze or measure retrieval-specific failure modes or sensitivity to retrieval quality (it does not implement retrieval-augmented episodic memory). Thus retrieval-specific constraints are plausible and can be inferred from the paper's discussion and broader literature, but they are not empirically characterized in this work.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper documents concrete limitations and failure modes of its approach: unfrozen fine-tuning leads to catastrophic forgetting of previously learned characters, pretraining bias can skew attention and hurt few-shot outcomes, and ablations show attention over raw logits outperforms attention over post-softmax probabilities.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory articulates retrieval-specific constraints: reduced benefit on tasks with no analogy/transfer potential (fully deterministic/fixed tasks) and heavy dependence of gains on retrieval quality (embeddings, weighting, window/plan selection).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Those retrieval-specific limitations are not discussed, measured, or analysed in the paper; the paper's documented constraints concern fine-tuning/attention design rather than retrieval-quality or analogy-dependence, so the theory's constraints are absent from this work.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly documents limitations that constrain applicability: reliance on a privileged valid-action list (valid-action aid) and on gold trajectories for behavior cloning, and notes that DRRN did not benefit from module injection in their experiments.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory specifies constraints specific to retrieval-augmented episodic memory, e.g., smaller gains for tasks with no analogy/transfer potential and sensitivity to retrieval quality (embeddings, weighting, selection of windows/plans).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Those retrieval-specific failure modes and constraints are not analyzed or tested in the paper; the paper’s listed limitations differ and do not address retrieval-quality or analogy-absence effects, so these constraints are novel relative to this work.

constraint_limitation.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper identifies practical limitations: LLMs struggle with very long/dynamic valid-action lists (bias to early entries), ToM benefits interact with TopK action reduction, agent performance depends on seating/position, and most LLMs without fine-tuning underperform a tuned RL agent (Danzero+).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory adds retrieval-specific constraints: reduced benefit in tasks with no transfer/analogy potential and sensitivity of effectiveness to retrieval quality (embedding, weighting, plan/window selection).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper documents several operational limitations of LLM-agents (action-list processing, TopK sensitivity, position effects), it does not analyze retrieval-quality-dependent failure modes or no-analogy regimes; those retrieval-centered constraints are not present in the paper and are therefore novel relative to it.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes long-term memory for LLM agents as a structured, dynamic world model combining semantic knowledge graphs with episodic observation vertices/edges, integrated into a cognitive loop of retrieval → planning → ReAct decision-making.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory presents a higher-level abstraction that memory architectures should be structured, task-specific, and polymodal (explicit KGs, retrieval-augmented episodic banks, and compressed representations) and that aligning memory structure to cognitive demands yields resource-efficient action selection and better generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: AriGraph concretely instantiates and validates the core reframing (integrated semantic+episodic KG) for textual interactive agents; the additional emphasis in the theory on polymodality and compressed summaries as equal pillars is a conceptual extension not fully realized within this single paper.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The survey presents a conceptual reframing: a unified reference architecture (memory, reasoning, perception–action), working vs long-term memory distinctions, and decompositions of memory design into consolidation, structuring, and retrieval. It organizes existing literature under this abstraction and maps game genres to cognitive demands.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes memory design as 'structured, task-specific, polymodal' architectures that should be matched to cognitive demands (planning, exploration, social reasoning), claiming that such targeted structuring yields specific downstream benefits (efficiency, robustness, generalization, multi-agent coherence).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already provides closely matching conceptual abstractions and explicitly argues for task-aligned memory design; the theory's terminology emphasizes the same organizing ideas and therefore largely reproduces the survey's conceptual reframing rather than introducing a new abstraction.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes LLM-agent design around a natural-language memory stream plus two derived, first-class abstractions: reflections (recursive abstractive syntheses forming a reflection tree) and hierarchical plans, and casts agent cognition as perceive→retrieve→reflect→plan cycles.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It formalizes treating reflections as stored memory objects (with pointers to evidence) and plans as retrievable memory entries, promoting retrieval and reflection as core cognitive operations feeding an LLM in interactive agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This conceptual reframing—treating episodic natural-language memories, reflection trees, and plans as the organizing abstraction for sustained LLM-based agents—is an explicit and central conceptual contribution of the paper.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work frames agent memory as vectors, summaries, chains of thought, workflows, or symbolic stores (databases/graphs); these representations have been used as retrieval indices.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: MemInsight reframes episodic memory as a collection of autonomously generated attribute-value annotations (with explicit perspective and granularity axes and priority ordering) and treats attributes as first-class retrieval indices that guide both filtering and embedding searches.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The explicit abstraction of memory entries as prioritized, perspective- and granularity-aware attribute annotations is a clear conceptual organization introduced by the paper; it is an operational reframing (organizing memory around attributes) rather than a fundamentally new theoretical ontology.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames response generation for game agents as a three-stage pipeline (Memory Retrieval → Self-Refinement → Self-Verification) and uses this decomposition as its core design abstraction.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: This decomposition is presented as a practical conceptual lens for building memory-augmented conversational agents in narrative multi-agent games and is validated experimentally through ablations and metrics.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the pipeline is a useful operational reframing, it is narrower than the broader theory's higher-level abstraction of 'structured, task-specific, polymodal memory architectures' (which includes knowledge graphs, compressed representations, etc.), so the paper's reframing is a focused, implementation-level contribution rather than the full conceptual taxonomy in the theory.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper adopts a two-tier memory framing—short in-trial trajectory window and longer-lived cross-trial reflection memory—and frames MC-DML as simulating human-like retention of recent details plus salient past experiences to guide planning inside MCTS.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory advances a higher-level abstraction advocating structured, task-specific, polymodal memory architectures (combining semantic/episodic graphs, retrieval banks, compressed summaries) as the organizing principle for improving LLM agent generalization, planning, and multi-agent coherence.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides the narrower two-tier reframing which aligns with part of the theory, but the broader conceptual prescription of a polymodal, task-specific memory taxonomy is not developed or instantiated in the paper and therefore is a peripheral extension.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames a four-component agent architecture (Memory, Reasoner, Retriever, Executor) and emphasizes retrieval-augmented planning as the central mechanism; it uses this architecture to operationalize episodic memory retrieval and in-context analogy-making.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes memory research into an explicit taxonomy of three complementary memory classes (explicit semantic/episodic knowledge graphs; retrieval-augmented episodic banks for analogical ICL; compressed/summarized representations for scaling) as an organizing abstraction for designing LLM agent memories.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That specific high-level taxonomy and reframing is not proposed or evaluated in the paper; while RAP's pipeline is a concrete instantiation consistent with one class (episodic banks), the broader conceptual taxonomy is an additional abstraction that must be derived from and validated beyond the paper's content.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper introduces a focused conceptual reframing: reflection should include positive experiences (Sweet) as well as negative ones (Sour) and these reflections should be managed in short- and long-term buffers for retrieval-enhanced decision-making.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: Sweet&Sour reframes the agent-reflection loop by elevating successful-subgoal reflections to first-class memory items that drive future planning, which is a conceptual shift from prior reflection work that focuses primarily on failures.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The broader theory's higher-level abstraction—categorizing memory needs into explicit knowledge graphs, retrieval-augmented episodic memory, and compressed/summarized representations as complementary, task-specific components—is not articulated or developed in the paper and therefore represents a conceptual extension beyond the paper's contribution.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes agent memory/state estimation as a learned, dynamic belief graph: it treats the belief graph as the agent's memory for partial-observability and planning, and formalizes graph updates (Δg_t) and decoding into a continuous adjacency tensor as the central abstraction.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory offers a broader conceptual reframing: 'memory architecture' should be seen as structured, task-specific, polymodal systems that explicitly combine semantic/episodic KGs, retrieval-augmented experience stores (for analogical in-context learning), and compressed summaries to meet different cognitive demands (planning, exploration, social reasoning).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper establishes the belief-graph-as-memory abstraction, it does not advocate or formalize the larger polymodal-memory abstraction combining retrieval banks and compression; that higher-level reframing is not present in the paper and represents a conceptual extension.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes policy improvement as 'verbal reinforcement learning' and formalizes a three-component abstraction (Actor, Evaluator, Self-Reflection) plus short-term vs long-term (verbal) memory; this conceptual lens is central to the paper's contribution.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a broader abstraction/taxonomy of memory architectures for LLM agents (explicit knowledge graphs, retrieval-augmented episodic memory, compressed/summarized representations) and argues for structuring memory to task-specific cognitive demands (planning, exploration, multi-agent coherence).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The Reflexion paper contains the core reframing (verbal RL and actor/evaluator/self-reflection loop) but does not develop the wider taxonomy of polymodal, task-specific memory architectures; that higher-level taxonomy is a new conceptual extension that can be derived from but is not explicitly formulated or validated in the paper.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper introduces a clear conceptual reframing: represent interaction history as a sequence of deltas (diffs) instead of raw full-text observations, providing a simple, deterministic, and task-agnostic abstraction that recasts LM 'memory' and emphasizes relational changes.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory offers a higher-level reframing that memory for LLM agents should be structured, task-specific and polymodal—decomposed into explicit KGs, retrieval-augmented episodic stores, and compressed summaries aligned to cognitive demands like planning, exploration, and social reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The diff-as-delta reframing is an explicit contribution of the paper and aligns with the broader idea that structured memory helps, but the specific tri-part architectural abstraction and cognitive-task alignment proposed by the theory are not developed in the paper and represent a novel, higher-level synthesis.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes LLMs as neurosymbolic reasoners and presents the conceptual move of treating symbolic modules as callable actions within a prompting/action-selection loop, which organizes how to deploy LLMs in symbolic text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory proposes a different conceptual abstraction: a taxonomy of agent memory modalities (explicit knowledge graphs, retrieval-augmented episodic banks, compressed summaries) and the principle that memory should be structured to task cognitive demands (planning, exploration, social reasoning).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That memory-centric taxonomy and the higher-level organizing principle linking specific memory modalities to cognitive demands is not present in the paper; the paper's reframing focuses on tool-augmented prompting rather than an explicit memory-architecture abstraction.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper introduces a clear conceptual reframing: distinguishing 'character' agents from 'actor/thespian' agents, and framing multi-character behavior as a policy decomposition problem solvable by per-character logits plus soft prompts and an attention-based blending module.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory offers a different high-level abstraction: treating agent memory as a structured, task-specific, polymodal architecture (knowledge-graph semantics/episodic stores, retrieval-augmented episodic memory for analogical in-context learning, and compressed summaries for scaling) and argues this memory design principle underlies improved planning, generalization, and multi-agent coherence.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's reframing is orthogonal and focused on policy decomposition and promptability rather than on designing polymodal memory architectures; the theory's memory-centered abstraction is not instantiated or argued in the paper.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper conceptually frames its contribution as a neurosymbolic engineering pattern: treating symbolic modules as injectable actions and using behavior cloning to teach transformers to exploit those modules, situating this within neurosymbolic and imitation-learning literature.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes augmentation as an organizing abstraction: 'structured, task-specific and polymodal memory architectures' (KGs, retrieval-augmented episodic banks, compressed summaries) as the central design principle for improving LLM agents' planning, exploration, and multi-agent coherence.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not advance or argue for that particular high-level taxonomy of memory modalities as the primary organizing principle; that conceptual reframing beyond neurosymbolic action injection is not present in the paper.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers a domain-level conceptual reframing: treat partially observed text worlds with an explicit knowledge graph as the core state abstraction and formulate language action generation as a constrained template-decoding problem guided by that graph, thereby turning an intractable natural-language action space into a tractable, structured decision problem.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes memory design more abstractly: memory should be structured, task-specific, and polymodal (semantic/episodic graphs, retrieval banks, compressed summaries) and aligned to cognitive demands (planning, exploration, social reasoning) to deliver resource-efficient action selection, robust recovery, and cross-task generalization for LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper contains a concrete operational reframing within IF (KG + template), but the higher-level abstraction advocating polymodal, task-tailored memory architectures and explicit retrieval-driven analogical memory for LLM agents is not present in the paper and therefore constitutes a novel conceptual reframing relative to it.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames role-playing behaviors as tasks and introduces a modular abstraction (frozen role-aligned expert policies + a trainable expert + an attention mixing mechanism) and treats a knowledge graph as structured external memory for state representation, using these abstractions to motivate transfer.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a broader conceptual taxonomy of agent memory—semantic and episodic knowledge graphs, retrieval-augmented episodic stores for analogical in-context learning, and compressed/summarized representations tailored to cognitive demands (planning, exploration, social reasoning)—as an organizing lens for designing LLM agents in text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Although the paper uses structured KGs and a modular policy decomposition, it does not articulate the multi-component memory taxonomy or the cognitive-alignment reframing (polymodal memory types matched to planning/exploration/social reasoning) that the theory advances; that higher-level conceptual reframing is absent from the paper.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper introduces two concrete conceptual reframings: (1) cast KG generation as predicting the difference between successive KGs, and (2) treat both graph triples and valid actions as permutation-invariant Sets-of-Sequences, leading to the SOS loss and corresponding architecture (dual encoders, joint decoders).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory offers a higher-level reframing: memory for LLM agents should be structured, task-specific and polymodal (explicit semantic graphs + episodic retrieval + compressed summaries) and organized to match cognitive demands (planning, exploration, social reasoning) rather than relying on monolithic context windows.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not introduce or operationalize this higher-level polymodal-memory abstraction; that conceptual abstraction and its proposed mapping to cognitive demands are new relative to the paper's contributions.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers a practical engineering abstraction (a modular pipeline separating state interpretation, ToM-based planning, evaluation, and external tool use) for deploying LLM agents in a complex card game, but it does not recast LLM-agent design around a unified memory-centric abstraction.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent design around 'structured, task-specific and polymodal memory architectures' as the central organizing abstraction—claiming memory architecture (knowledge graphs + episodic retrieval + compressed summaries) should be the primary design knob for planning, exploration, and social reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Elevating structured polymodal memory to a central conceptual abstraction is not present in the paper's framing; the paper remains planner/tool centric rather than proposing a memory-centric reorganization, so the reframing is novel relative to this work.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes unseen-game solving as a collaborative, multi-agent pipeline orchestrated by a tree-of-thoughts, turning reasoning and memory concerns into task-specialized agents and bounded-area representations; that reframing is a central conceptual contribution of the paper and is explicated and evaluated.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes LLM agent design around structured, task-specific, polymodal memory subsystems (semantic/episodic knowledge graphs, retrieval-augmented episodic memory, compressed/summarized representations) as the organizing abstraction for planning, analogy, and multi-agent coherence in text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's conceptual reframing is agent- and ToT-centric rather than memory-system-centric; the theory's proposed organizing abstraction (integrated polymodal memory tailored to tasks) is not developed in the paper beyond mentioning a future memory bank, so the memory-centric abstraction is new relative to this work.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames pretrained language models as action-generating agents and introduces the action modeling and iterated action modeling procedure as a practical training paradigm for text-based environments, but it does not propose a taxonomy of memory architectures.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent design around a structured memory taxonomy (explicit semantic/episodic graphs, retrieval-augmented episodic banks, and compressed/summarized representations) and links these memory types to specific cognitive demands (planning, exploration, multi-agent social reasoning).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This conceptual reframing (a structured, task-specific, polymodal memory architecture taxonomy and its mapping to agent capabilities) is not present in the paper and represents a new abstraction relative to the paper's engineering-focused contributions.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper operationalizes 'knowledge injection' as an engineering abstraction and distinguishes two knowledge types plus multiple injection strategies, thereby providing a pragmatic design-level taxonomy for augmenting agents in interactive text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory reframes memory for LLM agents at a higher conceptual level as a structured, task-specific, polymodal memory architecture composed of KGs, retrieval-augmented episodic stores, and compressed/summarized representations tied to cognitive demands (planning, exploration, social reasoning).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not elevate its findings into the same comprehensive conceptual taxonomy or argue the broader cognitive mapping the theory proposes; that higher-level reframing and prescriptive architecture is absent and therefore constitutes a novel conceptual contribution relative to this paper.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames the action-space problem as a language-model-based decompositional solution (learn an LM to propose context-conditioned candidate actions, then use an RL re-ranker), an applied architectural contribution rather than a broad taxonomy of memory subsystems.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent design around a taxonomy of structured memories—semantic/episodic knowledge graphs, retrieval-augmented episodic stores, and compressed/summarized representations—as the central organizing principle for improving LLM agents' planning, exploration, generalization and multi-agent reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That higher-level conceptual reframing and taxonomy of complementary memory modalities is not present in the paper; the paper's decomposition (proposer + re-ranker) is narrower and does not instantiate the proposed abstraction.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes pre-trained LMs as useful sources of transferable linguistic/world/game priors for RL agents in text games and contrasts this approach with explicit structured KGs, arguing that LM priors are an effective and simpler alternative in many cases.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a higher-level conceptual abstraction: treat agent memory as a composable, task-specific set of structured subsystems (semantic/episodic KGs, retrieval-augmented episodic memory, compressed summaries) chosen to match planning, exploration, and social-reasoning demands.
